props.text: We know very little about this part of the site but was probably used more intensively by the Late Woodland Culture (AD 400-1000). This culture emphasized hunting and gathering as well as initial domestication of plants like sunflowers and quinoa.

We know a fair amount about this part of the site, which is a small early Fort Ancient village (AD 1000-1300). This culture emphasized corn farming while continuing to hunt and gather. The stone mound you see near here was initially constructed during the earlier Late Woodland time but may have been intentionally integrated into this later village.
We know a fair amount about this part of the site. It appears to be very similar to Village 2, also being a small early Fort Ancient village (AD 1000-1300) with an emphasis on corn farming.

Turpin has been excavated several times starting in the 1800s. The first excavation was done by Harvard, then the Cincinnati Museum Center, and currently by Ohio State University and the University of Louisville.
Welcome to an interactive reconstruction of the Turpin archaeology site!

Follow the arrowheads on the ground making sure to stop at each numbered signpost. Click on the sound icons     on each signpost to learn about the site from many students and staff involved in the project. Start by using your phone to scan this QR code to watch a brief video that summarizes the bigger project entitled “Miami Valley Recollections and Reconnections”.
Thanks For Visiting! Scan this QR code to take a survey over the site.
